pain_point_label: Maintenance Logs and Asset Condition Tracking Are Siloed and Incomplete

matches.match_rationale: Maintenance logs currently exist in paper logbooks, handwritten notes, photos on phones, and mixed-format spreadsheets — all non-digital or semi-structured formats. OCR and document parsing can digitize this legacy and ongoing record-keeping into a structured, queryable asset history. For a multi-site education operator with no centralised CMMS, this is the essential first step before any analytics are possible.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A mobile-first digitization tool where maintenance staff photograph paper logs or handwritten repair notes; OCR extracts date, asset ID, work performed, technician, and cost into a structured database. Handles photos of broken equipment alongside the repair record.

matches.match_rationale: Each campus already has some maintenance data — in spreadsheets, different column naming conventions, inconsistent date formats, free-text asset descriptions — but it cannot be aggregated because schemas differ wildly. AI-assisted ETL can map heterogeneous campus-level data to a canonical asset register schema, normalizing asset IDs, maintenance codes, and cost fields without manual re-entry. This directly addresses the 'no standardized asset register' problem at group level.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An onboarding pipeline that ingests each campus's existing spreadsheets and maps them to a unified schema via LLM-assisted column matching and value normalization, producing a group-level asset register without requiring campuses to change their local tools immediately.

matches.match_rationale: Once maintenance histories are digitized, leadership and maintenance managers need synthesized condition summaries per asset or per campus — not raw log dumps. Summarization can distill years of repair records into a concise condition narrative ('this HVAC unit has had 4 compressor failures in 18 months, likely end-of-life') that supports capital planning decisions. This directly addresses the 'cannot answer which buildings are in worst condition' problem.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A condition briefing generator that, for any asset or building, synthesizes its full maintenance history into a one-paragraph condition assessment with key risk signals and recurrence patterns, formatted for leadership review or capital planning submissions.

matches.match_rationale: Recurring failures at the same asset are the clearest signal that a repair was inadequate or a root cause was missed — but siloed logs make this invisible. Anomaly detection on maintenance event time series can flag assets with abnormally high failure frequency or unexpectedly short repair intervals, surfacing recurrence patterns that would otherwise only be noticed anecdotally. This directly tackles the 'same failure occurs repeatedly because there is no learning from the prior repair' manifestation.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A recurrence-detection layer over the asset maintenance database that flags any asset where failure events cluster unusually close together or exceed campus baseline rates, alerting maintenance managers before the next failure rather than after.

matches.match_rationale: Leadership cannot prioritize capital investment because they lack condition intelligence — this is precisely a risk scoring problem. Using digitized maintenance history, asset age, repair frequency, and cost escalation trends, a risk model can produce an 'asset health score' for every piece of fixed equipment and every building, enabling ranked capital replacement planning. For a 500+ staff operator running multiple campuses, this replaces gut-feel with evidence-based asset risk stratification.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An asset risk dashboard that scores every major asset (HVAC, roofing, electrical systems, lifts) on a 1-100 failure risk index, updated as new maintenance events are logged, with a campus-level roll-up showing which sites carry the highest capital risk.

matches.match_rationale: When a system fails, maintenance teams cannot quickly retrieve relevant repair history to diagnose root cause — a classic retrieval + synthesis problem. A RAG system over the maintenance log corpus would let a technician ask 'what happened last time the boiler at Campus B failed in winter?' and get a grounded, cited answer from historical records rather than manually searching disparate files. This also supports warranty claims by surfacing documented evidence of prior reported failures.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A maintenance knowledge assistant accessible via mobile where technicians query asset history in natural language, receiving cited summaries of prior repairs, parts used, and contractor notes — reducing diagnostic time and enabling warranty claim documentation.

matches.match_rationale: Warranty claims and rectification requests are harder to support because work documentation is incomplete or informal. Text generation can produce formal, structured maintenance reports, warranty claim letters, and contractor rectification notices from raw log data — turning informal notes into professional documentation. Leadership capital planning submissions also benefit from AI-drafted condition reports grounded in the asset data.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A report-generation module that takes structured maintenance event data and produces a formal warranty claim letter or asset condition report in the format required by insurers, contractors, or the board — reducing admin burden on facilities managers.

matches.match_rationale: The full workflow from maintenance event capture to log entry to condition register update to manager notification involves multiple steps across systems and formats — a natural fit for a task automation agent. An agent can orchestrate: digitize incoming photo/note → extract structured fields → update asset register → check for recurrence patterns → trigger alert if threshold exceeded. This removes the manual discipline problem that causes logs to remain incomplete.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An always-on maintenance logging agent that monitors a shared inbox or mobile submission channel, automatically parses and classifies incoming maintenance records, updates the asset register, and sends confirmation receipts — making complete logging the path of least resistance for field staff.
